title: 7.5 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lesson 7.5!
topic_path: The Long Nineteenth Century (1750 to 1914 CE)

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the invention of the steam engine contribute to the shift from home-based textile production to factory-based manufacturing?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Steam engines were large and costly, so machines were grouped in shared factory spaces.
Cotton farming improved, so households produced more textiles to meet demand.
Machines ran on steam power, so human workers were no longer needed at home.
New machines required training, so work moved to places with skilled engineers.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains how *cultural* differences helped industrialization begin in Britain rather than China?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: British society valued individual experimentation and encouraged innovation.
Chinese scholars rejected all forms of science and refused to use machines.
Britain had no strong traditions or beliefs before industrialization began.
Chinese culture discouraged trade, so people avoided producing goods for markets.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why was building a national railroad system a crucial part of industrialization?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Railroads allowed factories to receive raw materials over long distances.
Railroads enabled people to travel faster, changing how society viewed distance.
Railroads helped spread new ideas and innovations between regions.
Railroads connected governments and improved political cooperation across countries.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What role did industrialization play in the expansion of European imperialism in the 19th century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It intensified imperialism as industrial nations sought raw materials, labor, and markets in colonies.
It delayed imperialism, as most European powers were focused on internal industrial development.
It launched imperialism as European powers sought to share industrial knowledge with other regions.
It led to the decline of imperialism by encouraging independence and global free trade.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did Britain’s financial strength contribute to its leadership in the Industrial Revolution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It allowed Britain to invest in new machines, factories, and infrastructure.
It helped Britain avoid all taxes and government regulations on businesses.
It gave Britain the power to force other countries to industrialize first.
It allowed Britain to keep all profits from trade within its own borders.

assessment_items.question_content:
**In what ways did Britain’s history of colonialism contribute to its leadership in the Industrial Revolution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Colonies provided raw materials and new markets for British manufactured goods.
Colonies forced Britain to focus only on agriculture instead of industry.
Colonial rule prevented Britain from trading with other European countries.
Colonies required Britain to abandon industrial development to manage overseas territories.

assessment_items.question_content:
Which factors related to the location of coal in Britain made it easier to mine and use during the early Industrial Revolution?

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Many coal deposits were close to the surface, reducing the need for deep mining.
Major coal reserves were located near growing cities, making labor and transport easier.
Coal was mostly found on islands far from population centers, so it was rarely used.
Coal was often located in dry desert areas, making extraction cheaper.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did changes in social class structure during the Industrial Revolution impact industrial growth?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The growing working class provided labor for factories, while a rising middle class invested in industry.
A decline in skilled artisans created a shortage of labor, slowing industrial progress.
The increase in wealth resulted in a growing middle and upper class, creating a labor shortage.
The working class left cities for farms, slowing down industrial production.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did Britain’s role as a major global trading power in the early 1800s support the development of industrialization?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Profits from global trade provided capital to invest in industrial growth.
Britain's strong trade network reduced the need for industrial development.
Britain's dependence on foreign goods forced it to industrialize.
A rise in imported manufactured goods slowed domestic industrialization.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was a key effect of increased middle-class representation in British Parliament during the Industrial Revolution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Laws were passed that supported industrial and economic growth.
Banks were required to fund new factory technologies.
Parliament introduced strong environmental protections for cities.
Worker protection laws were the main focus of new legislation.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the Industrial Revolution change social organization, especially in British society?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Extended family communities were replaced by smaller nuclear families with fewer support systems.
Families expanded to include extended relatives and neighbors as factory life encouraged communal living.
People formed strong social bonds in crowded cities that replaced the need for an extended family.
Industrialization ended family-centered living and promoted individualism over family structures.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What motivated British capitalists to invest in infrastructure in places like Russia and Argentina during the Industrial Revolution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They wanted to secure a growing supply of affordable wheat to feed Britain’s expanding urban workforce.
They hoped to discover gold reserves similar to those found in earlier Spanish colonies.
They wanted to export silver from British colonies to new markets in South America and Russia.
They aimed to gain military control over Atlantic shipping routes through strategic investments.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What role did copper and its production play in the Industrial Revolution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It was used in many industrial technologies and processes.
It was an important cash crop that bolstered European economies.
It was sometimes burned instead of coal to power steam engines.
It was important for harvesting tools used on sugarcane plantations.
